flashcards.front: What is the main challenge in the Producer-Consumer problem?
flashcards.back: Synchronizing access to a bounded buffer between producers (who add items) and consumers (who remove items) while preventing overflow and underflow.

flashcards.front: Name the three semaphores typically used in the Producer-Consumer solution.
flashcards.back: mutex (for mutual exclusion), full (counts full slots), empty (counts empty slots).

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between the first and second readers-writers problems?
flashcards.back: In the first variation, readers have priority (no reader waits unless writer is writing). In the second variation, writers have priority (writer proceeds as soon as possible).

flashcards.front: Why does the naive solution to the Dining Philosophers problem cause deadlock?
flashcards.back: Because all philosophers can simultaneously pick up their left chopstick, then all wait indefinitely for their right chopstick, creating circular wait.

flashcards.front: What synchronization construct is often used as a higher-level alternative to semaphores?
flashcards.back: Monitors, which encapsulate shared data and procedures with built-in mutual exclusion.
